visual_checks: Click and hold (mouse-down) on buttons, links, and interactive controls without releasing — does the action fire on the down-event before you release?
Check if dragging the pointer off an element after pressing down cancels the action when you release outside the target
Look for custom click/tap handlers on interactive elements that may fire on pointerdown or mousedown instead of click or pointerup
Identify any drag-and-drop interactions — these legitimately use the down-event but must allow reversal or abort
Test toggle buttons, dropdown menus, and modal triggers to see if they activate on press rather than on release
pass_conditions: Interactive elements fire their action on the up-event (mouseup, pointerup, click) rather than on the down-event
If the down-event is used to trigger functionality, a mechanism exists to abort (e.g., moving the pointer off the target before releasing) or to undo the action after completion
Drag-and-drop operations allow the user to cancel by releasing outside a valid drop zone, returning the item to its original position
Native HTML buttons, links, and form controls are used, which inherently fire on click (up-event) by default
fail_conditions: A button or link fires its action immediately on mousedown or pointerdown with no way to cancel by moving the pointer away
A custom interactive element triggers navigation or a destructive action on the down-event with no undo mechanism
A dropdown menu opens and immediately selects an item on the down-event without allowing the user to move away to cancel
A drag-and-drop operation commits the move with no way to abort mid-drag or undo after release
na_conditions: Page contains no interactive elements that respond to pointer input (no buttons, links, or custom click targets)
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Screenshots could not be captured for visual verification
Down-event versus up-event behavior cannot be determined from static analysis alone and requires interactive pointer testing
common_mistakes: Using mousedown or pointerdown event handlers for actions instead of the click event, which fires on release
Custom JavaScript widgets that fire actions on touchstart for perceived responsiveness, bypassing cancellation
Assuming that all browsers or frameworks handle pointer cancellation automatically — custom handlers can override default behavior
Drag-and-drop implementations that commit changes as soon as the user starts dragging, with no way to abort or undo
Overlooking third-party UI libraries that bind actions to down-events internally

examples.pass: A 'Delete' button activates on the click event (pointer release); if the user presses down but drags the pointer away before releasing, the action does not fire
examples.fail: A navigation link fires immediately on mousedown — the user accidentally touches it and cannot prevent navigation by sliding their finger away before lifting
examples.partial: Most buttons correctly use click events, but a custom 'quick action' toolbar fires actions on pointerdown with no abort or undo mechanism

plain_meaning: For any function operated by a single pointer, the platform must give users a way to prevent accidental activation. This means at least one of: the function does not activate on the initial down-event; there is a way to abort or undo; the up-event reverses any down-event outcome; or the down-event activation is essential. Most UIs satisfy this simply by acting on the up-event (click) rather than the down-event.
scope_applies_to: Buttons, links, and controls that activate on pointer interaction
Custom controls implemented with pointer event listeners
scope_does_not_apply_to: Essential down-event interactions (piano keys, real-time games, pressing a key in a typing test)
Controls whose behavior is determined by the user agent
borderline_calibration: Default click activation on up-event satisfies this SC automatically.
Only flag when the control demonstrably acts on pointerdown with no abort/undo path.
Do not flag common mouseup-based click handlers.

Static screenshots CANNOT determine whether pointer events fire on down-event or up-event. If the programmatic check found no mousedown/touchstart handlers in the script content, the SC likely passes. Do NOT flag elements based on visual appearance alone — pointer cancellation is a code-level concern.
